coredata.dc:title: Nanostructured metal oxide@carbon dots through sequential chitosan templating and carbonisation route
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Hammi, Nisrine
Marcotte, Nathalie
Marinova, Maya
Draoui, Khalid
Royer, Sebastien
El Kadib, Abdelkrim
coredata.dc:description: Pyrolytic conversion of biomass stands as one of the most promising sustainable routes to carbon-based nanostructures, including carbon dots (CDs). Yet, molecular composition of functionalized carbon dots is limited to heteroatom doping and the incorporation of few single metal precursors. Besides, most of these modifications were undertaken by strenuous post-grafting procedures, requiring organic solvents for carbon dispersion and excluding a large library of potential reactants because of incompatible experimental conditions. In a significant divergence from the well-established literature, we herein disclose an innovative and highly versatile approach for enriching carbon dot functionalities. This straightforward method merges chitosan as carbon precursor and metal alkoxide as ceramic precursor and explores a twin growth of two dissimilar "metal oxide@carbon dots" phases through hydrothermal conversion of the carbohydrate solution. Owing to the structure-directing effect of chitosan toward metal alkoxide precursors, a set of crystalline metal oxides including titanium dioxide, germanium oxide, and iron oxide clusters were intimately fused within the in situ formed nitrogen-containing carbon framework. Distinctively, the following approach uses water as solvent and renewable biomass as carbon source and is expected to shed light on the hidden talent of discarded bio-waste for engineering functional nanomaterials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chitosan
Porous carbon
Carbon dots
Sol-gel
Hydrothermal synthesis

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: PHC Toubkal (PHC 18/70) and (PHC 19/87) (CNRST-CNRS) are acknowledged for financial support. NH thanks UEMF for scholarship. AEK thanks UEMF for research funding. AEK and NM thank Fondation Balard. SR acknowledges Chevreul institute (FR 2638), Ministère de l'Enseignement Supérieur et de la Recherche, Région Nord – Pas de Calais and FEDER for supporting and funding partially this work. The authors thank Thomas Cacciaguerra (Institut Charles Gerhardt Montpellier) for SEM analyses.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the sequential approach developed in this study. Aqueous colloidal chitosan solution is mixed with metal alkoxide species through sol-gel chemistry. Hydrothermal treatment allows simultaneously the formation of the metal oxide phase and the carbonisation of chitosan biopolymer.

Characterization of CS@C and MOx@C nano-hybrids. (a-d) XRD of MOx@C and the ICDD reference of the corresponding crystalline MOx phase. (e-h) Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms obtained for CS@C and for MOx@C materials. (i-l) SEM images of the corresponding materials.

Characterization of MOx@CD nanohybrids. STEM-EDX images of (a). TiO2@CD; (b). GeO2@CD; (c). Fe2O3@CD. For each hybrid, mapping the localisation of carbon, nitrogen and the corresponding metal (Ti, Ge and Fe) confirms the homogeneous distribution of the two phases.

(a) UV–visible absorption and PL excitation (λem = 400 nm) and emission (λex = 310 nm) spectra of CS@CD. Inset: Photographic image of CS@CD in day light and under UV-light irradiation at 365 nm. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of CS@CD at different excitation wavelengths.

head.ce:title: Nanostructured metal oxide@carbon dots through sequential chitosan templating and carbonisation route

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Pyrolytic conversion of biomass stands as one of the most promising sustainable routes to carbon-based nanostructures, including carbon dots (CDs). Yet, molecular composition of functionalized carbon dots is limited to heteroatom doping and the incorporation of few single metal precursors. Besides, most of these modifications were undertaken by strenuous post-grafting procedures, requiring organic solvents for carbon dispersion and excluding a large library of potential reactants because of incompatible experimental conditions. In a significant divergence from the well-established literature, we herein disclose an innovative and highly versatile approach for enriching carbon dot functionalities. This straightforward method merges chitosan as carbon precursor and metal alkoxide as ceramic precursor and explores a twin growth of two dissimilar "metal oxide@carbon dots" phases through hydrothermal conversion of the carbohydrate solution. Owing to the structure-directing effect of chitosan toward metal alkoxide precursors, a set of crystalline metal oxides including titanium dioxide, germanium oxide, and iron oxide clusters were intimately fused within the in situ formed nitrogen-containing carbon framework. Distinctively, the following approach uses water as solvent and renewable biomass as carbon source and is expected to shed light on the hidden talent of discarded bio-waste for engineering functional nanomaterials.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon-based materials are of great interest owing to the combination of a set of unusual characteristics including thermal and chemical stability, hydrophobicity and hardness, electrical conductivity and optical properties (Titirici et al., 2015). The attractiveness of different carbon allotropes (e.g. fullerene, carbon nanotubes and graphene) allows the replacement of heavy metal nanoparticles by their light-weight carbon congeners and their massive implementation in fundamental nanoscience and applied nanotechnology (Deng et al., 2016). The serendipitous discovery that a side-product of carbon nanotubes contained nano-sized fluorescent impurities has triggered efforts for the synthesis of the so-called carbon dots (CD) (Xu et al., 2004). This new variant has emerged for many reasons including its unexpected fluorescence, good photostability against bleaching and blinking, low cost and good biocompatibility (Hutton et al., 2017). Evidently, one of the most convincing selling points of carbon dots is their organic nature, making them apparently less toxic with respect to their inorganic quantum dots competitors. These advantages render CDs promising contenders in the challenging area of nanomedicine as fluorescent markers, bioimaging and biosensor agents, in renewable energy and in wearable electronic technologies (Liu et al., 2020). Consequently, setting versatile and cost-effective methods for the scalable synthesis of carbon dots presents a tremendous interest.

A recent trend explores the transformation of biomass and bio-waste into carbon dots (Meng et al., 2019). Diverging from the earliest physical methods, this wet chemistry provides carbon nanoparticles in large quantities through straightforward and easy-to-run experimental procedures (Baker et al., 2010). This trivial approach constitutes a major driving force for the substantial growth of research revolving around carbon dots (Choi et al., 2020). Among the naturally abundant precursors, chitosan stands as one of the most attractive carbonaceous skeleton owing to its availability as marine fishery waste (Yan et al., 2015), its nontoxicity and the possibility for shaping its body in different forms including nanostructured films, configurable hydrogels, hierarchically porous microspheres and crack-free monoliths (El Kadib et al., 2012). Besides, chitosan features nitrogen groups in its backbone, which allow the preparation of nitrogen-containing carbon materials (Khan et al., 2020; Primo et al., 2012; Zhao et al., 2010; (Wang et al., 2016)) without using exogenous nitrogen sources (ammonia, urea, triazine, folic acid, haemoglobin, protein, etc.) that may add cost, complicate the procedure and alter the biocompatibility of the final carbon material (Hammi et al., 2020). The benefits of nitrogen-doping prompted the incorporation of other elements like phosphorus, sulphur and boron (Xu et al., 2016; (Wang and Su, 2014)). Single metals (Ag, Au, Rh) and semiconductor salts (ZnS, ZnO) were also post-synthetically incorporated in these carbon dot structures (Pakkath et al., 2018). In contrast and to the best of our knowledge, no report has hitherto disclosed the in situ preparation of metal oxide@carbon dots (MOx@CD) from soluble, well-defined molecular or macromolecular precursors. This class of bifunctional materials is of great interest for tailoring synergistic electron transfer between two dissimilar components (Luo et al., 2020). For instance, outstanding performance was recorded in ZnO-based solar cells when carbon dots are used as sensitizers (Briscoe et al., 2015). Herein, we describe a one-pot synthesis of three nanohybrids including TiO2@CD, GeO2@CD and Fe2O3@CD through a straightforward and sustainable route. This approach operates under hydrothermal treatment, using water as the sole solvent and a marine polysaccharide waste (chitosan, CS) simultaneously acting as a template, and carbon and nitrogen sources. Noteworthy, few synthetic methodologies of this versatility and chemical diversity attractiveness are currently known.

Ti(OiPr)4, Titanium (IV) diisopropoxide bis (acetylacetonate) (Ti(acac)2OiPr2), Iron (III) acetylacetonate (Fe(acac)3), Germanium (IV) ethoxide (Ge(OEt)4), absolute ethanol, acetic acid (99%) were obtained from Sigma Aldrich, and used without any further purification. Chitosan (low, medium, high molecular weight) with a deacetylation degree of 85% were supplied by Sigma Aldrich and used as received.

Fourier transformed infrared spectra were obtained with a Perkin Elmer Spectrum 100 FT-IR spectrometer on neat samples (ATR FT-IR). X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded on a D8 Advance Bruker AXS system using Cu Kα radiation with a step size of 0.02° in the 2θ range from 0.3° to 10° for SAXS and from 5° to 87° for WAXS (geometry: Bragg−Brentano, θ/2θ mode). DRUV spectra were measured in the 200−800 nm range using Spectralon as the reference on a PerkinElmer Lambda 40 spectrometer equipped with an integrating sphere (Lapshere, North Sutton, NH). Nitrogen sorption isotherms at 77 K were obtained with a Micromeritics Tristar apparatus. Prior to measurement, the sample was evacuated for 9 h under vacuum at 120°C. The surface area (SBET) was determined from BET treatment in the relative pressure range corresponding to P/P0 < 0.4, and assuming a surface coverage of nitrogen molecule of 13.5 Å2. Pore diameters were determined from the desorption branches using the BdB method. Steady state fluorescence experiments were performed on a multi-frequency phase fluorometer (K2-001, ISS, Champaign, USA) at right angle geometry using 10 mm quartz cells (Hellma). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements were carried out with an Autosizer 4800 instrument (Malvern, U.K.) using a 50 mW laser operating at 532 nm. Hydrodynamic diameters (Dh) were obtained by fitting the intensity autocorrelation function by the method of cumulants. Scanning electronic microscopy (SEM) images were obtained using a JEOL JSM 6700F. HRTEM images were collected on a TITAN Themis 300 S/TEM with a probe aberration corrector and monochromator, allowing spatial resolution of 70 pm and energy resolution of 150 meV. The microscope was equipped with a super-X windowless 4 quadrant SDD (silicon drift detector) detection system for the STEM-EDX mapping and with several annual dark field detectors (HAADF). Thermogravimetric analyses (TGA) were obtained with a thermal analyser instrument Q500 on the range 25 – 1000°C at heating rate of 5°C/min. The Raman spectroscopy work was carried out on a Raman SENTERRA system (Bruker) equipped with a laser emitting at 785 nm, using an integration time of 20 s and a laser power of 10 mW at the source, in the 200 to 1200 cm−1 range.

0.1g of chitosan was dissolved in 16 mL of aqueous acetic acid (1% v/v). An alcoholic solution of the titanium alkoxide precursor with different molar ratio of NH2:Ti (1:1; 2:1; 4:1) was added and the mixture was stirred for 1 hour at room temperature to obtain a homogeneous dispersion. The resulting solution was poured into a Teflon autoclave with a capacity of 20 mL. The Teflon autoclave was sealed and heated at 180°C for 24 h. The product obtained after the hydrothermal reaction was centrifuged at a high speed (10000 rpm) for 10 min, and then the supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 µm filter membrane. The obtained precipitate was a nitrogen-doped carbon hybrid material, denoted as TiO2@C, and the supernatant was a nitrogen doped carbon dot hybrid material, denoted as TiO2@CD. Similar procedure was also applied for the synthesis of GeO2@C and Fe2O3@C using Ge(OEt)4 and Fe(acac)3 respectively. For the sake of comparison, non-modified carbon dots were prepared by hydrothermal treatment of chitosan without adding any metal precursor. The resulting material was denoted as CS@C.

Our screening experiments showed that the highest isolated mass of CS@C was obtained through hydrothermal treatment (180°C for 24 h) of low molecular weight chitosan, compared to medium and high molecular weight biopolymer (Table S1a). We have therefore considered this one for our hybrid material synthesis. At a first glance, we focused on TiO2@C owing to the abundance of literature discussing key parameters in sol-gel mineralisation of titanium alkoxide and considering our gathered knowledge in controlling titanium dioxide growth and crystallisation through chitosan templating route (El Kadib et al., 2010; (El Kadib et al., 2011) (El Kadib et al., 2014); (El Kadib, 2020)).

The targeted material was prepared through a multistep procedure where an alcoholic solution of Ti(OiPr)4 was added to a colloidal solution of chitosan dissolved in acetic acid aqueous solution (Fig. 1
, Experimental section and Table S1b). The resulting homogeneous solution was hydrothermally treated at 180°C for 24h. The solution turned black and the harvested solid material denoted as TiO2@C was structurally and texturally analysed using a set of complementary techniques.

FTIR analysis of CS@C and its parent precursor chitosan witnesses the significant changes occurring on the polysaccharide skeleton during the hydrothermal treatment, in consistency with the resulting black colour (Fig. S2-a). FTIR of TiO2@C is quite similar to CS@C, with some minor variations, including the presence of a signal at 965 cm−1 assignable to the mineral phase and a significant decrease of the broad hydrogen bonding band of OH and NH groups centred at ~3400 cm−1 (Fig. S2-b). This indicates a consumption of OH groups in TiO2@C with respect to CS@C pointing to the possible grafting of the metal-oxo-alkoxide species on the carbonaceous framework during the hydrothermal treatment (El Kadib et al., 2011).

X-ray diffraction (XRD) of CS@C shows a broad peak around 23° (Fig. 2
a), very reminiscent to the broad amorphous peak in the starting biopolymer (Ospina et al., 2015). This indicates that the treated carbonaceous skeleton is built from a disordered carbon network rather than truncated sheets (Yang et al., 2012). XRD profile of TiO2@C reveals the appearance of a set of peaks unambiguously attributed to anatase crystalline phase (Fig. 2 b) (Tsung et al., 2008). Besides, increasing the molar ratio of Ti(OiPr)4 with respect to NH2 of chitosan resulted in a substantial increase of the diffraction intensity, indicating increased crystallinity of the metal oxide phase grown on the carbonised framework (Fig. S4a). The size of anatase particulates is as small as 8.2 nm, as estimated by Scherrer equation. The crystal size does not vary by changing the Ti(OiPr)4 precursor to Ti(OiPr)2(acac)2, despite the well-known difference in their hydrolysis and condensation kinetics (Hammi et al., 2019). Thus, the strong chelating ability of chitosan toward Lewis acidic metal alkoxide (NH2→Ti) restricts to some extent the polymerisation growth of the metal alkoxide while the hydrothermal treatment induces polymer decomposition and carbonisation. This sequential procedure results in twin growth of metal oxide phase and carbon framework in the same tectonic nanostructure (El Kadib, 2016) . Notably, while anatase crystals routinely form only under harsh solid-state treatment conditions, often at T > 450°C (El Kadib et al., 2011), our hydrothermal conditions provide enough energy for converting the initially formed amorphous titanium dioxide bursts into crystalline anatase phase irrespective to the NH2-to-metal ratio (Fig. 2 b, Fig. S4). Similar triggered crystallisation from liquid-phase was previously reported in confined medium using ionic liquids (Łuczak et al., 2017), amphiphilic dendrimers (Brahmi et al., 2015) and water-exfoliated graphene sheets (Tan et al., 2012) among other synthetic nano- and micro-reactors. The small size of ~ 8 nm of the resulting clusters can be rooted in the entrapment of titanium-oxo-alkoxy species within the in situ-carbonized network, which efficiently hinder the crystal expansion above the nanometer regime. Notably, the involvement of the graphitised carbon network in controlling faceted growth of a set of metal nanoparticles has been recently evidenced (Primo et al., 2016; Primo et al., 2015). In Raman spectra, the peaks observed at 148, 398, and 626 cm−1 for the TiO2@C composite are consistent with the formation of crystalline anatase nanoparticles (Fig. S5b) (Alamelu et al., 2018). Additionally, two characteristic peaks at 1373 cm−1 and at 1596 cm−1 are respectively attributed to D and G bands of the graphitized network (Primo et al., 2014), with a ID/IG ratio being equal to 0.86. Comparatively, the corresponding peaks in CS@C are observed at 1352 cm−1 and 1566 cm−1 respectively for D and G, with an ID/IG ratio of 0.82 (Fig. S5a). The as-discussed ratio reflects the ordering/disordering and the crystalline/amorphous nature of the carbon network (Štengl et al., 2013). The width of the two peaks reflects a poor graphitisation of the carbon framework and its amorphous nature. The shift observed for TiO2@C in D and G bands and the slight ID/IG increase compared to CS@C are consistent with the covalent anchorage of titanium alkoxide species on the edge of the carbon framework (Anouar et al., 2019; Benjwal et al., 2015). These species act as nucleating sites for further growth of anatase nanoparticles on the top of the carbon layer and as cross-linkers for building the intimately mixed hybrid material network, thereby enhancing its disordering.

XPS measurements were further performed to elucidate the electronic environment of carbon, nitrogen and titanium species (Fig. S6). Typical carbon signals assignable to C-C/C=C, C-N and C-O are observed at 284.8, 286.3 and 288.1 eV. Deconvolution of N (1s) spectrum (Fig. S6c) reveals the presence of three peaks at 398.8, 400 and 401.5 eV characteristic of nitrogen embedded in graphene network. Nitrogen species embedded in the framework of carbon materials could exist in different forms including amino, pyridinic, pyrrolic and pyridine-like, graphitic and nitroso forms depending mainly on the pyrolysis temperature (Yamada et al., 2014). Considering that the hydrothermal treatment was undertaken below T = 200°C, the presence of a large portion of pyridinic and graphitic nitrogen species is quite unlikely as it commonly requires high temperature exceeding T > 700°C (Lavorato et al., 2014). The major peak at 400.0 eV can be ascribed to nitrogen coordinating Lewis acidic sites (N→Ti-O), its position being very reminiscent to the one previously observed for the binding energy of NH2 of chitosan upon coordination with Lewis acid species (NH2 →M) (Pakkath et al.,2018, El Kadib et al., 2010, 2011, 2012) and the replacement of oxygen by nitrogen in the crystal lattice of TiO2 (Ren et al., 2015). Decomposition of the Ti (2p) signal reveals the presence of two peaks at 458.6 and 464.5 eV assignable to Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2, respectively (Fig. S6d). The Ti 2p orbit scan of TiO2@C nanocomposite exhibits a 0.5 eV shift toward lower binding energies compared with pristine TiO2 (Jiaa et al., 2018). The presence of nitrogen in the outer-sphere coordination of Ti induces partial charge transfer, commonly referred as ligand-to-metal charge transfer (LMCT). In the whole, XPS spectrum provides salient evidence for the formation of carbon-like graphene network and the presence of both nitrogen species and titanium in proximal distance, thereby ruling the possible hybrid heterogeneity through phase separation or segregation (Xing et al., 2009).

SEM analyses reveal that carbonised CS@C is built from interconnected primary microsphere units (Fig. 2 i-S3a). Such porous network is generated during the thermal degradation of chitosan and its subsequent graphitisation. The first step involves the destruction of the crystalline regions between adjacent chains, followed by the hydrolysis of the pyranose skeletal and dehydration-cyclisation that allow the formation of extended conjugated network. A slightly different network is observed for TiO2@C, which displays a denser and uniform surface probably due to the coating of the as-formed carbon network by titanium dioxide (Fig. 2 j-S3b). Nitrogen adsorption-desorption of CS@C reveals substantial nitrogen uptake, with a specific surface area of 75 m2.g−1 and an average pore diameter of 16 nm. The isotherm profile is typical of large mesopores that extend to the macropores domain with the presence of interparticle voids (Fig. 2 e). The TiO2@C displays a hysteresis loop typical of open mesoporous network, with a specific surface area of 94 m2.g−1, an average pore diameter of 11.5 nm, and a pore volume of 0.27 cm3.g−1 (Fig. 2 f).

To gain further insight on the stability of TiO2@C, its thermal degradation profile was plotted against native CS@C. For the two materials, the weight loss begins with water evaporation that allow further chemical condensation and network consolidation followed next by the simultaneous- decomposition of carbonaceous species and mineralisation/volatilisation of organic species (Fig. S7). A significant increase of T50 (50 wt.% decomposition temperature) was observed from 466°C for CS@C to 523°C for TiO2@C, owing to the incorporation of inorganic clusters. While total degradation and mineralisation was experienced for CS@C, the hybrid TiO2@C keeps 48 wt.% of its weight at 800°C, which correspond to the incorporated amount of titanium dioxide. This amount is almost consistent with the ratio engaged in the initial gel mixture (1:1 ratio of Ti:NH2). This thermal stability enhancement is of tremendous interest and has to be considered when ultra-stable carbon-based nanomaterials are required.

To broaden the library of accessible functional carbon dots, we further investigated this dual templating-carbonisation approach using other sol-gel processable precursors, including Fe(acac)3, and Ge(OEt)4 (Table S1b). In the special case of GeO2@C, well-truncated mesocrystals assignable to germanium oxide are observed by SEM (Fig. 2 k-S3d). XRD reveals typical diffraction peaks of hexagonal germanium oxide (Seng et al., 2012), from which a crystal size of 71 nm is estimated, thereby corroborating our hypothesis on the formation of large nanometric single crystals (Fig. 2 c). Concomitantly, nitrogen sorption analysis shows the formation of an open porous network, with a specific surface area of 71 m2.g−1, an average pore diameter of 19.7 nm and a pore volume of 0.35 cm3.g−1 (Fig. 2 g). Hydrothermal treatment of Fe(acac)3 in chitosan solution was also performed. XRD of the harvested solid displays peaks typical of well-resolved Fe2O3 crystalline phase with an average size of ~46 nm (Fig. 2 d). In this case, homogeneous primary building blocks are observed (SEM of Fig. 2 l-S3c), while the presence of an open porous network (SBET = 56 m2.g−1, Dpore=25.7 nm and Vpore=0.22 cm2.g−1) was confirmed through nitrogen sorption analyses (Fig. 2 h).

Subjecting the harvested mixture to ultracentrifugation produces a transparent solution denoted as CS@CD in the case of native chitosan and MOx@CD when metal alkoxide were mixed with chitosan. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements show that native CS@CD display particles with an average size around 3 nm (Fig. S8a). Comparatively, native TiO2@CD displays soluble nanoparticles with an average size of ~ 9 nm, while highest average size was noticed for GeO2@CD (58 nm) and Fe2O3@CD (50 nm) (Fig. S8b-d). TEM analyses show that TiO2@CD network is built from mostly spherical dots (Fig. S9). Statistical analyses of more than 80 dots reveals an average size of about 6 nm (Fig. S10a). HRTEM nicely shows the presence of small, monodisperse hybrid nanodots where a lattice fringe of 0.34 nm, can be recognized from the photos, and attributed to [002] plane of graphitic carbon. Concomitantly, a second lattice fringe at 0.23 nm, assignable to [004] plane of crystalline anatase phase is also observed (Fig. S10b,c). The homogenous distribution of C, N, and Ti in the whole network was confirmed through STEM-EDX mapping bringing a salient evidence for the successful synthesis of intimately fused nano-sized metal oxide@carbon dots without phase separation or segregation (Fig. 3
a). Similar pattern of nanoscale distribution of the metal oxide and nanocarbon phase occurs also for GeO2@CD and Fe2O3@CD thereby validating the versatility of our approach to access a large molecular diversity of metal oxide ceramic@carbon dots (Fig. 3 b-c).

UV-Vis absorption spectroscopy of CS@CD reveals an absorption band at 286 nm typically assigned to the π→π* electronic transitions of the aromatic π system (Fig. 4
a). Also seen are the n→π* transitions of carbonyl groups, characterized by a weak shoulder at ~320 nm that extends towards longer wavelengths [45]. CS@CD aqueous solutions appear slightly yellowish brown under daylight and exhibit a strong blue light emission when exposed to UV irradiation (λ = 365 nm). Upon excitation at 330 nm, the photoluminescence (PL) spectrum shows an intense band at 402 nm. Changing the excitation wavelength from 310 to 350 nm significantly increases the intensity of the 402 nm emission band, whereas varying it from 370 to 400 nm redshifts the emission band from 415 to 442 nm and sharply decreases the intensity (Fig. 4 b). Such excitation dependant PL behaviour is similar to that reported for other carbon dot (Jing et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2016; s (Baker et al., 2010; Yang et al., 2012; Zhan et al., ). It may arise from the selective excitation of various sized CD, different emissive trap sites on the CD's surface and diverse fluorescent polyaromatic surface species formed during the hydrothermal treatment (Sun et al., 2006). Interestingly, doping with the metal oxides retains the emissive properties of CD (Fig. S11). Whereas TiO2@CD (Fig. S11a) and GeO2@CD (Fig. S11b) exhibit the same intensity trend as CS@CD, Fe2O3@CD interestingly show a continuous increase of the emission band intensity all over the excitation range spanned (from 310 to 400 nm). A redshift difference was also observed between CD (λem = 445 nm, 447 nm and 454 nm for TiO2@CD, GeO2@CD and Fe2O3@CD, respectively upon excitation at 400 nm). These findings point out the known complexity of CD optical properties that still need to be elucidated and the potent role of the various metals on the emissive surface defects (Guo et al., 2018; Tang et al., 2012) that may be advantageously used to produce particles with tuneable optical properties.

Tremendous interest was recently devoted to the synthesis and surface functionalisation of carbon framework including the so-called carbon dots. Diverging from the ubiquitously reported post-grafting procedures, we herein report a sustainable approach based on sequential chitosan templating, metal oxide growth and crystallisation and biopolymer carbonisation. The advantages imparted by the use of chitosan-derived marine waste as a carbohydrate framework lies in: i) its abundance and cost-effectiveness, ii) its rich framework functionalities that allow templating various nano-objects and iii) its carbonisation under metal-free conditions. The interplay of chitosan with metal alkoxide in the starting solution is a key enabler to reach an intimate dispersion at the nanoscale for the resulting metal oxide@carbon dot hybrid materials. The disclosed approach is sustainable, highly-versatile and allows the preparation of a large library of discrete crystalline metal oxide-carbon dots from well-defined, soluble molecular and macromolecular precursors. These hitherto elusive nanomaterials open new possibilities for tailoring and engineering synergies in bifunctional metal oxide@carbon nanohybrids.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: PHC Toubkal (PHC 18/70) and (PHC 19/87) (CNRST-CNRS) are acknowledged for financial support. NH thanks UEMF for scholarship. AEK thanks UEMF for research funding. AEK and NM thank Fondation Balard. SR acknowledges Chevreul institute (FR 2638), Ministère de l'Enseignement Supérieur et de la Recherche, Région Nord – Pas de Calais and FEDER for supporting and funding partially this work. The authors thank Thomas Cacciaguerra (Institut Charles Gerhardt Montpellier) for SEM analyses.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.carpta.2021.100043.
